00043 #include <types.h>
00044 #include <kern/errno.h>
00045 #include <syscall.h>
00046 #include <kern/syscall.h>
00047 #include <lib.h>
00048 #include <mips/trapframe.h>
00049 #include <thread.h>
00050 #include <current.h>
00051 #include <copyinout.h>
00052 #include <kern/file.h>
00053 
00054 
00055 /*
00056  * System call dispatcher.
00057  *
00058  * A pointer to the trapframe created during exception entry (in
00059  * exception.S) is passed in.
00060  *
00061  * The calling conventions for syscalls are as follows: Like ordinary
00062  * function calls, the first 4 32-bit arguments are passed in the 4
00063  * argument registers a0-a3. 64-bit arguments are passed in *aligned*
00064  * pairs of registers, that is, either a0/a1 or a2/a3. This means that
00065  * if the first argument is 32-bit and the second is 64-bit, a1 is
00066  * unused.
00067  *
00068  * This much is the same as the calling conventions for ordinary
00069  * function calls. In addition, the system call number is passed in
00070  * the v0 register.
00071  *
00072  * On successful return, the return value is passed back in the v0
00073  * register, or v0 and v1 if 64-bit. This is also like an ordinary
00074  * function call, and additionally the a3 register is also set to 0 to
00075  * indicate success.
00076  *
00077  * On an error return, the error code is passed back in the v0
00078  * register, and the a3 register is set to 1 to indicate failure.
00079  * (Userlevel code takes care of storing the error code in errno and
00080  * returning the value -1 from the actual userlevel syscall function.
00081  * See src/user/lib/libc/arch/mips/syscalls-mips.S and related files.)
00082  *
00083  * Upon syscall return the program counter stored in the trapframe
00084  * must be incremented by one instruction; otherwise the exception
00085  * return code will restart the "syscall" instruction and the system
00086  * call will repeat forever.
00087  *
00088  * If you run out of registers (which happens quickly with 64-bit
00089  * values) further arguments must be fetched from the user-level
00090  * stack, starting at sp+16 to skip over the slots for the
00091  * registerized values, with copyin().
00092  */
00093 
00094 
00095 
00096 void
00097 syscall(struct trapframe *tf)
00098 {
00099         int callno;
00100         int32_t retval;
00101         int err,whence,tmp;
00102         off_t offset;
00103 
00104         KASSERT(curthread != NULL);
00105         KASSERT(curthread->t_curspl == 0);
00106         KASSERT(curthread->t_iplhigh_count == 0);
00107 
00108         callno = tf->tf_v0;
00109 
00110         /*
00111          * Initialize retval to 0. Many of the system calls don't
00112          * really return a value, just 0 for success and -1 on
00113          * error. Since retval is the value returned on success,
00114          * initialize it to 0 by default; thus it's not necessary to
00115          * deal with it except for calls that return other values, 
00116          * like write.
00117          */
00118 
00119         retval = 0;
00120 
00121         switch (callno) {
00122 
00123             case SYS_reboot:
00124                 err = sys_reboot(tf->tf_a0);
00125                 break;
00126 
00127             case SYS___time:
00128                 err = sys___time((userptr_t)tf->tf_a0,
00129                                  (userptr_t)tf->tf_a1);
00130                 break;
00131 
00132             /* Add stuff here */
00133             case SYS_write:
00134                         err = sys_write((int)tf->tf_a0, (const void*) tf->tf_a1, (size_t) tf->tf_a2, (int*)&retval);
00135                         break;
00136 
00137             case SYS_open:
00138                 err = sys_open((const char*)tf->tf_a0, (int)tf->tf_a1, (int*)&retval);
00139                 break;
00140 
00141             case SYS_close:
00142                 err = sys_close((int) tf->tf_a0);
00143                 break;
00144 
00145             case SYS_read:
00146                 err = sys_read((int)tf->tf_a0, (const void*) tf->tf_a1, (size_t) tf->tf_a2, (int*)&retval);
00147                 break;
00148             case SYS_dup2:
00149                 err = sys_dup2((int)tf->tf_a0, (int)tf->tf_a1, (int*)&retval);
00150                 break;
00151           case SYS_lseek:
00152                 /* Building 64bit offset from two 32 bit fields */
00153                 offset = (off_t) (tf->tf_a2) ;
00154                 offset = (offset<<32) | (tf->tf_a3);
00155                 
00156                 /* Read third-parameter from user-level stack*/
00157                 tmp=(tf->tf_sp)+16;
00158                 copyin ((const_userptr_t)tmp,(void*)(&whence), sizeof(int));
00159         
00160                 err = sys_lseek((int)tf->tf_a0, (off_t)(tf->tf_a3),(int) whence,(int*)&retval);
00161                 
00162                 break;
00163               case SYS_fork:
00164                 err = sys_fork(tf,(int*)&retval);
00165                 break;
00166               case SYS_getpid:
00167                 err = sys_getpid((int*)&retval);
00168                 break;
00169           
00170               case SYS_waitpid:
00171                 err = waitpid(tf->tf_a0, (int *)tf->tf_a1, tf->tf_a2, &retval);
00172                 break;
00173               case SYS_execv:
00174                 err = sys_execv((const char *)tf->tf_a0, (char **)tf->tf_a1);
00175                 break;
00176             case SYS__exit:
00177                 _exit((int)(tf->tf_a0));
00178                 break;
00179  
00180             default:
00181                 kprintf("Unknown syscall %d\n", callno);
00182                 err = ENOSYS;
00183                 break;
00184         }
00185 
00186 
00187         if (err) {
00188                 /*
00189                  * Return the error code. This gets converted at
00190                  * userlevel to a return value of -1 and the error
00191                  * code in errno.
00192                  */
00193                 tf->tf_v0 = err;
00194                 tf->tf_a3 = 1;      /* signal an error */
00195         }
00196         else {
00197                 /* Success. */
00198                 tf->tf_v0 = retval;
00199                 tf->tf_a3 = 0;      /* signal no error */
00200         }
00201         
00202         /*
00203          * Now, advance the program counter, to avoid restarting
00204          * the syscall over and over again.
00205          */
00206         
00207         tf->tf_epc += 4;
00208 
00209         /* Make sure the syscall code didn't forget to lower spl */
00210         KASSERT(curthread->t_curspl == 0);
00211         /* ...or leak any spinlocks */
00212         KASSERT(curthread->t_iplhigh_count == 0);
00213 }
00214 
00215 /*
00216  * Enter user mode for a newly forked process.
00217  *
00218  * This function is provided as a reminder. You need to write
00219  * both it and the code that calls it.
00220  *
00221  * Thus, you can trash it and do things another way if you prefer.
00222  */
00223 void
00224 enter_forked_process(struct trapframe *tf)
00225 {
00226 
00227         tf->tf_v0 = 0;
00228         tf->tf_a3 = 0;
00229         tf->tf_epc += 4;
00230         
00231         mips_usermode(tf);
00232 
00233 }
Generated on Sun Jun 27 22:12:13 2010 for Os/161 by  1.6.1
